intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The uAgents Framework Runtime is model-agnostic Python plumbing (`uagents` + `uagents-core`) that gives every deployed agent a cryptographic identity and a Fetch.ai wallet, registers its address and endpoints on the Almanac contract, and exchanges typed, cryptographically signed messages with other agents over an authenticated HTTP endpoint. Its declared security duty is to hand each agent a protected key identity, authenticated and replay-resistant messaging, schema-validated inputs, and bounded, observable operation. Private keys, wallet keys and seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext; administrative, introspection and message-history interfaces MUST NOT be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers; and binding the server to a public interface or enabling the inspector are actions that require operator approval.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python framework built on `uvicorn`/ASGI, `pydantic` v2, `ecdsa` (SECP256k1) and `cosmpy` for the Fetch.ai wallet and ledger. Inbound envelopes arrive at `/submit` in `src/uagents/asgi.py`, are pydantic-validated, signature-verified for agent senders, and routed by `dispatch.py` to typed developer handlers; identity, signing and verification live in `uagents-core/uagents_core/identity.py` and `envelope.py`. The ASGI server binds `HOST = "0.0.0.0"` unconditionally with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, and `Agent(enable_agent_inspector=True)` is the default, registering `/messages`, `/connect`, `/disconnect` and `/agent_info` REST endpoints guarded only by a client-IP loopback check. Keys are seed-derivable from an operator-supplied seed (env-only, no disk write), but the seed-less named path in `storage/__init__.py` persists identity and wallet private keys to a plaintext `private_keys.json` in the working directory.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a framework that offers safe primitives — real ecdsa envelope signing and verification, deterministic schema validation, and seed-derived env-only keys — while its default runtime posture undoes them. The finding to lead with is a compound chain: the server binds to `0.0.0.0` on every interface with the agent inspector enabled by default, and the only guard on the `/messages`, `/connect` and `/disconnect` administrative endpoints is a `"127.0.0.1" in scope["client"]` check that `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` makes spoofable with a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header — so a remote, unauthenticated caller can read an agent's full message history and re-register or unregister it. Two independent gaps deepen the exposure: inbound signed envelopes carry `nonce` and `expires` fields that are signed but never enforced on receipt (no replay or expiry rejection), and the seed-less key path writes identity and wallet private keys to disk in cleartext.

rules.rule_text: Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Alert Operator (Do Not Halt)
rules.rule_text: The agent server is bound to a public interface.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

rules.section: Known Good Baseline — Normal Restart Cadence
rules.rule_text: Restarts must not change a seeded agent's identity or wallet address.

findings.summary: Compound chain lets a remote unauthenticated caller reach the agent's admin and message-history endpoints by spoofing a loopback source address.
findings.description: The server binds all interfaces and enables the inspector by default, exposing `/messages`, `/connect` and `/disconnect`; their only guard is a `"127.0.0.1" in scope["client"]` check that `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` makes spoofable via a forged `X-Forwarded-For` header. A remote caller can therefore read full message history and re-register or unregister the agent in Agentverse. Fixing the public bind removes the reachability that makes the spoofable guard and default-on inspector exploitable.
findings.policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers. / Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers. / Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.

evidence.snippet: reserved-endpoint guard at lines 304-311 `request_path in RESERVED_ENDPOINTS-{"/agent_info"} and "127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]` — spoofable because serve() sets forwarded_allow_ips="*" at :184, trusting X-Forwarded-For from any client
enable_agent_inspector defaults True (:305) and registers /messages (:488-494, returns full cached message history), /connect and /disconnect (:496-531)

recommended_actions: Default the server bind to `127.0.0.1` and require an explicit operator opt-in (and warning) to bind a public interface; do not set `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` unless a trusted proxy is configured.
Default `enable_agent_inspector` to False and gate the reserved admin endpoints behind an authenticated token rather than a spoofable client-IP check.

findings.summary: The seed-less named-agent path writes identity and wallet private keys to disk in cleartext, which the remit forbids outright.
findings.description: `get_or_create_private_keys` persists the agent identity key and wallet key as plaintext JSON in the working directory via `save_private_keys`, and `Agent._initialize_wallet_and_identity` takes this path whenever a `name` is set without a `seed`. The wallet key controls on-chain funds, so a leaked or committed `private_keys.json` is a direct identity and asset compromise. There is no encryption or key-manager option on this path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form. / Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: save_private_keys writes {"identity_key", "wallet_key"} to plaintext private_keys.json in cwd (:113-127); get_or_create_private_keys calls it (:130-150) — note :149 also saves a fresh PrivateKey() rather than the returned wallet_key
_initialize_wallet_and_identity uses get_or_create_private_keys(name) when seed is None and name is set (:585-592), making plaintext persistence the default for that path

recommended_actions: Encrypt keys at rest (OS keychain, an encrypted keystore, or a secret manager) or restrict this path to an operator-supplied env-only seed; never write wallet/identity keys as cleartext JSON.
Fix the `save_private_keys(name, identity_key, PrivateKey().private_key)` call at `storage/__init__.py:149` so the persisted wallet key matches the returned one.

findings.summary: The ASGI server always binds to 0.0.0.0 with no loopback option, approval gate, or operator alert, exposing every agent publicly by default.
findings.description: `HOST` is the hardcoded constant `"0.0.0.0"` and `Agent` exposes no host parameter, so the server binds all interfaces on every deployment regardless of intent. The remit requires operator approval to bind a non-loopback interface and an operator alert when it happens; the framework provides neither. This is the standalone exposure that makes the admin-endpoint chain (PRAX-2026-07-17-001) remotely reachable, but it also exposes `/submit` publicly on its own.
findings.policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface. / The agent server is bound to a public interface.

evidence.snippet: module-level `HOST = "0.0.0.0"` (:23) passed to uvicorn.Config(host=HOST, ...) in serve() (:181) with no override path
Agent.__init__ accepts port but no host/bind parameter (:292-356), so a loopback default is impossible without editing the framework

recommended_actions: Add a bind-host parameter defaulting to `127.0.0.1`, requiring an explicit opt-in for a public bind, and emit a startup warning to the operator when a non-loopback interface is selected.

findings.summary: Inbound signed envelopes are never checked for expiry or replay, so a captured valid envelope can be re-delivered and re-processed.
findings.description: The `Envelope` carries `nonce` and `expires` fields that are folded into the signed digest, but the inbound ASGI and mailbox paths verify only the signature and then dispatch — neither compares `expires` against the current time nor tracks `nonce`/session to reject a duplicate. A recorded valid envelope replays cleanly, which the remit lists as never-allowed. The `expires` field is consulted only to size a sync-response timeout, not to reject stale envelopes.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh. / Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

evidence.snippet: after env.verify() (:363-365) the handler calls dispatcher.dispatch_msg (:379-385) with no env.expires-vs-now check and no nonce/session replay cache
_digest() signs nonce and expires (:107-110) but no runtime consumer enforces them on receipt; mailbox.py:268-287 shows the same verify-then-dispatch pattern

recommended_actions: Reject envelopes whose `expires` is in the past before dispatch, and track a bounded per-session set of seen `nonce`/digest values to drop replays.

findings.summary: Any sender whose address starts with the `user` prefix skips signature verification, so the sender identity handlers receive is attacker-chosen.
findings.description: The inbound path verifies signatures only when `is_user_address(env.sender)` is false, and `is_user_address` merely checks the literal `user` prefix. Unauthenticated user queries are in scope by the remit, but nothing binds a claimed `user...` address to any proof, so an attacker can assert an arbitrary user identity and any per-sender handler logic keyed on it is trust-on-assertion. Impact is bounded because agent-to-agent messages are still verified; the risk is spoofed user identity reaching handlers.
findings.policy_rule_text: Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity. / Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

evidence.snippet: `if not is_user_address(env.sender): env.verify()` — user-prefixed senders bypass verification entirely before dispatch
is_user_address returns True on a bare `user` string-prefix match with no cryptographic binding to the sender

recommended_actions: Document that `user`-prefixed senders are unauthenticated and provide a first-class way for handlers to distinguish verified agent senders from unverified user senders before any security decision.

findings.summary: A failed envelope signature verification is logged at warning and answered with a 400, but the agent neither halts nor alerts the operator as the remit escalation requires.
findings.description: The remit classifies a signature-verification failure as a halt-and-alert event, but the framework logs a warning line and returns a 400, then continues serving with no durable record or operator notification. Against console-only default logging this leaves a security-relevant event easy to miss and impossible to alert on.
findings.policy_rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

evidence.snippet: verification failure path logs `self._logger.warning("Failed to verify envelope...")` and returns status 400 (:366-371); no halt, no structured alert, no durable audit entry

recommended_actions: Emit a structured, durable security event on verification failure and provide an operator-configurable alert or halt hook rather than only a console warning.

findings.summary: The server sends `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with wildcard methods and headers on every response, letting any web origin script requests to the agent.
findings.description: `serve()` hardcodes wildcard CORS headers on all responses, so a page on any origin loaded in a victim's browser can issue cross-origin requests to the agent's endpoints. `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials` is set to `false`, which prevents credentialed requests and bounds the impact, but combined with the default public bind the API is scriptable from arbitrary web origins with no configuration hook to restrict it.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn.Config headers set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*", Allow-Methods "GET, POST, OPTIONS", Allow-Headers "*" on every response (:185-190); no origin allowlist option

recommended_actions: Make the allowed CORS origin configurable and default it to same-origin/none rather than `*`.

findings.summary: The wallet/ledger dependency `cosmpy` is floor-pinned with no upper bound and no lockfile is committed, leaving install-time reproducibility and version-swap exposure.
findings.description: Most dependencies carry bounded `>=x,<y` ranges, but `cosmpy` — the library that holds wallet and ledger key handling — is declared `>=0.12.1` with no upper bound, so a future breaking or malicious release is pulled in unbounded. No lockfile is committed for reproducible installs. For a library this floor-pin style is conventional, but the unbounded security-critical dependency is the flaggable gap.

evidence.snippet: dependencies pin `cosmpy (>=0.12.1)` with no upper bound while peers use bounded ranges (uagents-core >=0.4.8, pydantic >=2.8,<3.0, uvicorn >=0.30.1,<1.0); no lockfile committed

recommended_actions: Add an upper bound to the `cosmpy` constraint and commit a lockfile (uv.lock / poetry.lock) so security-critical dependency versions are reproducible.

positives.title: Cryptographic envelope signing and verification on the agent path
positives.description: Agent-to-agent envelopes are signed with an ecdsa SECP256k1 identity and verified against the claimed sender address before dispatch, a real and operative trust control.

positives.title: Outbound envelopes signed and time-bounded
positives.description: Every outbound message is signed with the agent identity and stamped with an `expires` timestamp before delivery.

positives.title: Deterministic schema validation before dispatch
positives.description: Inbound payloads are validated against the registered pydantic message schema before any handler runs, rejecting malformed input at the framework boundary.

positives.title: Seed-derived env-only key derivation
positives.description: When an operator supplies a seed, identity and wallet keys are derived deterministically in memory with no disk write, keeping identity stable across restarts — the recommended non-plaintext path.

positives.title: Opt-in rate-limiting and access-control primitive
positives.description: The framework ships a `QuotaProtocol` with per-sender rate limits and allow/block ACLs that a developer can attach to handlers, though it is off by default.

rows.purpose: server startup, registration lifecycle, and failed-envelope-verification warnings
same records routed to a file only when the developer passes a log_file path

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The framework ships a genuine cryptographic core — SECP256k1 envelope signing and verification, deterministic pydantic schema validation, and seed-derived env-only keys — but its default runtime posture systematically undercuts it: the server binds to all interfaces with the inspector enabled and its admin guard spoofable, inbound envelopes carry no replay or expiry enforcement, and the convenience key path writes private keys to disk in cleartext. Adversarial testing of the trust boundary and durable, action-level auditing are effectively absent. The safe primitives are present; the defaults handed to every deployed agent are not.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The runtime restricts inbound traffic to `/submit`, reserved admin paths and developer-registered REST handlers, and dispatches only pydantic-schema-validated typed messages, but any signed agent may invoke any handler by default and the rate-limit/ACL layer (`experimental/quota`) is opt-in and off, so domain scoping exists but is incomplete.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Inbound payloads are validated against registered pydantic message schemas before dispatch (`asgi.py` model_validate), a genuine deterministic input control, but the framework draws no trusted/untrusted content distinction and dispatches unauthenticated user-sender messages to handlers with equal trust.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Envelope signature verification for agent senders is real and operative (`envelope.verify` via `identity.verify_digest` at `asgi.py:363-365`), lifting this above ad hoc, but replay/expiry is unenforced, the admin loopback guard is spoofable under `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, the server binds `0.0.0.0` by default, and user-prefixed senders bypass verification — key gaps remain.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Component versions are declared (`uagents` 0.25.3, `uagents-core` 0.4.8) and most dependencies carry bounded `>=x,<y` ranges, but `cosmpy` is floor-pinned with no upper bound in `pyproject.toml` and no lockfile is committed for reproducible installs.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: The test suite (`tests/test_msg_verify.py`, `test_server.py`, `test_registration.py`) exercises functional happy-path behavior only; a search for replay, spoofing, injection, forgery or tampering across `tests/` returns no hits, so there is no evidence of adversarial testing of the trust boundary.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Logging is console `logging.basicConfig(INFO)` in `utils.py` with an optional file handler only when a developer supplies `log_file`; failed signature verifications are logged at warning but there is no structured, durable, action-level audit record by default.
